blocks.statement: I can describe Oersted's 1820 discovery (compass deflects near a current-carrying wire), use the right-hand rule to predict B-field direction around a wire, and explain why a coil of wire (solenoid) acts like a bar magnet.

blocks.connection: Today: CURRENTS also create B-fields. This was the discovery that unified electricity and magnetism — and made the Car motor (Day 15) possible.

blocks.markdown:
## The accident that unified two sciences

In 1820, Hans Christian Oersted was lecturing in Copenhagen with a battery circuit on the bench. A compass happened to sit near the wire. When he closed the circuit, the needle **swung away from North** — and snapped back when he opened it. The conclusion was enormous: **electric currents create magnetic fields.** Before that moment, electricity and magnetism were thought to be separate subjects. After it, they were one: electromagnetism.

The geometry: a straight wire's B-field wraps in **circles around the wire**. To find the direction, use the **right-hand rule**: point your thumb along the current I, and your fingers **curl in the direction of B**.

Now wrap that wire into a coil — a **solenoid**. Each loop's circular field adds to its neighbors', and the stacked result looks exactly like a bar magnet's field, with an N end and an S end. Strength scales with (number of turns) × (current): more turns, more current → stronger electromagnet. Switch the current off and the field vanishes — a magnet with an OFF switch. That controllable magnet is the heart of doorbells, relays, speakers, and the DC motor you'll build with in Unit 8.

terms.definition: Hans Christian Oersted accidentally noticed: a compass needle DEFLECTS when held near a wire carrying current. Conclusion: electric currents create magnetic fields. Example: the compass needle aligned WITH the new magnetic field, not with Earth's. Removing the current → needle returns to North. This was the first link between electricity and magnetism.
terms.cognate: Sp. descubrimiento de Oersted · Pt. descoberta de Oersted · HC dekouvèt Oersted

terms.definition: To find direction of B around a wire: point thumb in direction of current I, fingers CURL in direction of B field. Example: current pointing UP: B circles the wire counterclockwise as viewed from above. Current pointing into the page (×): B circles clockwise as viewed from in front.
terms.cognate: Sp. regla de la mano derecha · Pt. regra da mão direita · HC règ men dwat

terms.definition: A wire wrapped into a coil. When current flows, the individual loops' B-fields add up, producing a B-field much like a bar magnet — with N and S poles at the ends. Example: doorbells, relays, electromagnets, MRI machines, the speakers in your phone, the inside of a DC motor — all are solenoids in disguise. Strength depends on (number of turns) × (current) × (μ₀); switching off current = field vanishes.
terms.cognate: Sp. solenoide · Pt. solenoide · HC solenoyid

blocks.title: ALWAYS the right hand
blocks.markdown: ALWAYS use your right hand. A left-hand rule does the opposite — confusion-inducing. Even if you're left-handed, the right-hand rule is the convention.

blocks.instruction: Three parts: (A) straight wire with concentric circular B-lines, (B) right hand showing thumb-along-I, fingers-curling-B, (C) coiled wire = bar-magnet-like B-field.
prompts: (A) Straight wire, concentric B circles around it.
(B) Right hand: thumb = I, curling fingers = B.
(C) Solenoid with a bar-magnet-shaped field, N and S labeled.

blocks.prompt: DIRECTION OF B AROUND A WIRE — A long straight wire carries current flowing UP. A compass is placed to the EAST of the wire. Which way does the compass needle deflect?
blocks.given: I points UP · compass is to the EAST of the wire
blocks.equation: Right-hand rule: thumb UP → fingers curl from N → E → S → W → N (counterclockwise viewed from above)
blocks.work:
To the EAST of the wire, the B field points SOUTH (since fingers curl from East toward South).
Compass N pole aligns with B → compass needle points SOUTH.

blocks.prompt: SOLENOID POLARITY — A solenoid (coiled wire) is wound so that — viewed from one end — the current flows COUNTERCLOCKWISE. Use the right-hand rule (curl fingers along current, thumb points to N pole) to determine which end of the solenoid is N.
blocks.givenHint: Viewed from one end, current circulates counterclockwise.
blocks.equationHint: Coil version of the rule: curl your fingers along the current; your thumb points out of the N pole.
equationOptions: Curl fingers along current → thumb points to N
Point thumb along current → fingers point to N

blocks.title: ★ Half of electromagnetism — the other half is Day 14
blocks.markdown: Oersted's discovery is the OPENING of electromagnetism. Day 14 will give the REVERSE: changing B-field creates VOLTAGE. Together, these two laws (Oersted + Faraday) make every motor, every generator, every transformer, and every wireless charger work.

blocks.prompt: A straight wire has current flowing to the RIGHT. Above the wire, which direction does B point? (Use right-hand rule.) Show or describe your work.
blocks.frame: Oersted discovered that currents create ___ fields. The right-hand rule says to point your ___ in the direction of current, then your fingers curl in the direction of ___. A coiled wire (solenoid) acts like a ___.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **36.5–36.8** (currents make magnetic fields, forces on moving charges and wires, and meters to motors). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
